patient.communicationStyle: Provides clear timeline. Concerned about having passed out at work. Downplays ongoing dyspnea initially.
patient.emotionalState: Embarrassed about fainting, increasingly anxious about breathing difficulty

responses.opening: I fainted at work about two hours ago. I was just standing up from my desk and the next thing I knew, I was on the floor. My coworkers called 911. I've also been a little short of breath the last couple days, but I figured it was just stress.
responses.severity: The fainting was sudden — I didn't even feel it coming. Now I'd say my breathing is maybe a 5 out of 10 bothersome. It's worse when I walk to the bathroom.
responses.location: I don't really have pain per se. Maybe a little tightness in my chest when I breathe deeply.
responses.onset: I'd say the shortness of breath started maybe three or four days ago. Gradually. The fainting was today, just the one time.
responses.character: It's more like I can't catch my breath. If I sit still I'm okay, but if I try to do anything I get winded really fast. That's not normal for me.
responses.worse: Walking, going up stairs, taking a deep breath. I also feel worse when I'm lying completely flat.
responses.better: Sitting still helps. I feel okay right now just sitting here.
responses.radiation: No real pain to talk about.
responses.timing: The breathing has been getting gradually worse over a few days. The fainting was out of nowhere.

chiefComplaint.location: No focal pain; mild pleuritic chest tightness
chiefComplaint.onset: Syncope sudden; dyspnea gradual over 3-4 days
chiefComplaint.duration: LOC lasted approximately 30 seconds per witnesses
chiefComplaint.timing: Syncope occurred on standing from seated position at desk

present.name: dyspnea on exertion
present.patientSays: I've been getting winded just walking around the office the past few days.

present.name: pleuritic chest tightness
present.patientSays: There's a little tightness when I take a deep breath, like a catch.

present.name: tachycardia sensation
present.patientSays: My heart's been racing. I can feel it pounding sometimes.

present.name: left calf pain/swelling
present.patientSays: Actually, yeah — my left leg has been kind of achy and swollen for about a week. I thought I pulled something at the gym.

present.name: lightheadedness
present.patientSays: I've felt a little dizzy the last day or so, even before I fainted.

absent.name: hemoptysis
absent.patientSays: No, I haven't coughed up any blood.

absent.name: fever
absent.patientSays: No fever that I know of.

absent.name: orthopnea
absent.patientSays: I don't usually need extra pillows.

absent.name: seizure activity
absent.patientSays: My coworker said I didn't shake or anything. I just went down and woke up.

medications.name: Combined oral contraceptive pill
medications.patientSays: I've been on the pill for years.

medications.name: Sumatriptan
medications.patientSays: I take it when I get a migraine, maybe once a month.

socialHistory.alcohol: Social, 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends
socialHistory.occupation: Marketing manager, desk job — sits 8+ hours/day
socialHistory.travel: 10-hour flight back from Europe about 10 days ago

familyHistory: Mother: DVT after hip surgery at age 62

physicalExam.general: Alert, mildly anxious, tachypneic at rest, no acute distress when sitting still
physicalExam.heent: No oral trauma (no tongue bite), pupils equal and reactive
physicalExam.neck: No JVD (may be present if significant RV strain)
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, loud P2 (pulmonic component), no murmurs
physicalExam.pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Tachypneic.
physicalExam.extremities: LEFT calf: 3cm circumference difference vs right, mildly erythematous, tender to palpation, pitting edema. Right leg normal. Positive Homan's sign on left (low sensitivity).
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert and oriented x4, no focal deficits, no post-ictal features

findings: S1Q3T3 pattern (S wave in lead I, Q wave in lead III, inverted T wave in lead III)
T-wave inversions in V1-V3 (right ventricular strain pattern)
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia with right heart strain pattern — concerning for PE

labs.note: Respiratory alkalosis with hypoxemia — A-a gradient elevated

imaging.findings: Large saddle embolus at the bifurcation of the main pulmonary artery with extension into bilateral lobar and segmental arteries. Enlarged RV with RV/LV ratio >1.0. Interventricular septal bowing toward LV.
imaging.impression: Massive bilateral pulmonary embolism with right ventricular strain

imaging.findings: Mild cardiomegaly. No focal consolidation. Subtle Westermark sign (oligemia) in right lower lobe.
imaging.impression: Findings suggestive of pulmonary embolism. Recommend CTPA.

imaging.findings: Non-compressible left popliteal vein and proximal posterior tibial vein. Echogenic thrombus visualized.
imaging.impression: Acute DVT of the left popliteal and posterior tibial veins

onCorrectTreatment.vitalsTrend: HR decreases to 90s, BP stabilizes at 110s/70s, SpO2 improves to 95% on 2L NC
onCorrectTreatment.symptomsTrend: Dyspnea gradually improving over 24-48 hours, no further syncope
onCorrectTreatment.narrative: Heparin drip initiated. Given hemodynamic instability, interventional radiology consulted for catheter-directed thrombolysis. Patient monitored in ICU. Gradual improvement over 24 hours.

onDelayedTreatment.vitalsTrend: BP drops to 80s/50s, HR increases to 130s, SpO2 drops to 85%
onDelayedTreatment.narrative: Patient becomes increasingly hypotensive and obtunded. Obstructive shock from massive PE. Requires vasopressors and emergent systemic thrombolysis.

keyLearningPoints: PE is a must-not-miss cause of syncope — always consider it
Wells criteria: DVT symptoms, HR>100, immobilization/surgery, prior VTE, hemoptysis, malignancy, PE most likely diagnosis
Massive PE = hemodynamic instability → systemic thrombolysis or catheter-directed therapy
Submassive PE = RV dysfunction (elevated troponin/BNP, RV dilation on CT) without shock
Sinus tachycardia is the most common ECG finding in PE; S1Q3T3 is classic but not sensitive
Clear lungs + hypoxemia + tachycardia = think PE
mustNotMiss: Cardiac arrhythmia (get ECG — look for long QT, Brugada, WPW)
Aortic stenosis (syncope on exertion, systolic murmur)
Ruptured ectopic pregnancy (reproductive-age women, abdominal pain)
commonPitfalls: Attributing syncope to vasovagal without looking for red flags
Not examining the legs — unilateral leg swelling is a critical clue
Relying on a normal chest X-ray to exclude PE
D-dimer is a rule-out test only — elevated D-dimer is nonspecific

expertDifferential.why: Syncope + tachycardia + hypoxemia + unilateral leg swelling + risk factors (OCP, long flight, sedentary, family hx)
Standing trigger, but persistent tachycardia/hypoxemia argue against
Possible, but ECG shows sinus tachycardia, not arrhythmia
Positional trigger, but ongoing symptoms suggest more than simple orthostasis
No witnessed tonic-clonic activity, no post-ictal state, no tongue bite or incontinence

teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutLegs: Excellent — the asymmetric leg swelling is the key clue. Always examine the calves in syncope workup.
teachingHooks.ifMissesLegSwelling: Patient may unconsciously rub or favor the swollen leg. Ask: 'Have you noticed any swelling or pain in your legs?'
teachingHooks.ifJumpsToVasovagal: Vasovagal is common but a diagnosis of exclusion. What red flags should make you pause? Persistent tachycardia? Hypoxemia?
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutOCPHistory: Great question — combined oral contraceptives increase VTE risk 3-4x, especially with other risk factors like immobility.
teachingHooks.ifOrdersDDimer: D-dimer is elevated. But in this patient, your pre-test probability is already high — you could argue for going straight to CTPA.
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutTravel: The 10-hour flight was key. Prolonged immobility + OCP = compounding risk factors.

patient.communicationStyle: Embarrassed about fainting. Very forthcoming when asked about triggers. Quick to reassure you they feel fine now.
patient.emotionalState: Embarrassed, mildly anxious, wants to go home

responses.opening: I fainted at the blood drive at school. I was sitting in the chair donating blood and started feeling really dizzy and nauseous, and the next thing I knew people were putting cold towels on my face. I feel fine now though.
responses.severity: I'm really fine now. When it happened it was scary, but the feeling passed once they laid me down and gave me juice.
responses.onset: It was right in the middle of the blood draw. Maybe 5 minutes in? I started feeling warm and lightheaded.
responses.worse: I think the sight of the needle and blood bothered me more than I expected.
responses.better: I feel totally normal now. They laid me down and I recovered in a few minutes.

chiefComplaint.onset: During blood donation, preceded by prodrome (warmth, nausea, tunnel vision)
chiefComplaint.duration: LOC approximately 15-20 seconds, full recovery within 2-3 minutes

present.name: prodrome (warmth, nausea, tunnel vision)
present.patientSays: Right before, I felt really warm and nauseous. My vision got kind of tunnely.

present.name: diaphoresis (before event)
present.patientSays: I was sweating a lot right before it happened.

present.name: rapid full recovery
present.patientSays: Within a couple minutes of lying down I felt totally normal.

absent.name: palpitations
absent.patientSays: No, my heart wasn't racing before.

absent.name: seizure activity
absent.patientSays: They said I didn't shake or anything.

absent.name: prior episodes
absent.patientSays: Actually, I did almost faint once in high school when I got blood drawn. Same kind of thing.

physicalExam.general: Well-appearing young adult, no acute distress, mildly pale
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, no displaced PMI
physicalExam.extremities: No edema, no calf tenderness, symmetric
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert and oriented x4, no focal deficits, normal gait

keyLearningPoints: Classic vasovagal: identifiable trigger + prodrome + rapid recovery = low risk
San Francisco Syncope Rule and Canadian Syncope Risk Score help risk-stratify
Normal ECG + no red flags = reassurance and discharge with education
Red flags for syncope: exertional, no prodrome, abnormal ECG, family hx sudden death, structural heart disease
Vasovagal is the most common cause of syncope but remains a diagnosis of exclusion
mustNotMiss: Long QT syndrome (check QTc on ECG)
Arrhythmia (ask about palpitations, family history of sudden death)

expertDifferential.why: Clear trigger (needle/blood), classic prodrome, rapid recovery, no red flags, normal ECG
Overlapping mechanism — blood draw as trigger
Always on differential, but normal ECG and classic vasovagal story makes this unlikely
Possible if fasting before donation, but glucose is normal
